(Chinese)|12=draft}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=香香背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|12=draft}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|12=draft}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|12=draft}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|12=draft}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|12=draft}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|12=draft}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|12=draft}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|12=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|12=draft}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|12=draft}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|12=draft}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|12=draft}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|12=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|12=draft}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|12=draft}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|12=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|12=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|12=draft}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|12=draft}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|12=draft}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|12=draft}} == Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Hitachi City History (Hitachi-shi){{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} == External Links == * [http://omikajinjya.sakura.ne.jp Ōmika Jinja] (Official Site) * {{Twitter|mikaboshi77|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Facebook|ohmikajinjya|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Instagram|omikajinja|Ōmika Jinja}} {{神道 横}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shizuri Faith}} [[category:Completion tier 1]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Ibaraki Prefecture shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Hitachi City]] [[Category:History of Hitachi City]] [[Category:translated pages]] [[Category:Shizuri Faith]] [[Category:Shizuri Faith new pages]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Ōmika Shrine]] [[ja:大甕神社]] [[de:Große Mikaschrein]]

shrine|de|Shinto shrine (German)|ja|神社|zh|Shinto shrine (Chinese)|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|12=draft}}. == Enshrined Kami == * Main Enshrined Kami – {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto |lt_ja=建葉槌命 |lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German) |lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |fr|12=draft}} (たけはづちのみこと) * Local Tutelary – {{ill|Kagaseo (Mikaboshi)|de|Kagaseo (German)|ja|天津甕星|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi|2=zh|3=天津甕星|4=ja|5=天津甕星|6=ru|7=Амацумикабоси|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|zh|Kagaseo (Chinese) |lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=甕星香々背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|12=draft}} === About the Enshrined Kami === According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|de|Nihon Shoki (German)|ja|日本書紀|zh|Nihon Shoki (Chinese)|12=draft}} (Age of the Gods section), the two deities enshrined at {{ill|Katori Jingū|de|Katori Jingū (German)|ja|香取神宮|zh|Katori Jingū (Chinese)|12=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Shimousa Province|de|Shimousa Province (German)|ja|下総国|zh|Shimousa Province (Chinese)|12=draft}}) — {{ill|Futsunushi|de|Futsunushi (German)|ja|経津主神|zh|Futsunushi (Chinese)|12=draft}} — and at {{ill|Kashima Jingū|de|Kashima Jingū (German)|ja|鹿島神宮|zh|Kashima Jingū (Chinese)|12=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Hitachi Province|de|Hitachi Province (German)|ja|常陸国|zh|Hitachi Province (Chinese)|12=draft}}) — {{ill|Takemikazuchi|de|Takemikazuchi (German)|ja|タケミカヅチ|zh|Takemikazuchi (Chinese) |lt=Takemikazuchi |lt_ja=武甕槌神 |lt_de=Takemikazuchi (German) |lt_zh=Takemikazuchi (Chinese)|12=draft}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=香香背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|12=draft}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|12=draft}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|12=draft}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|12=draft}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|12=draft}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|12=draft}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|12=draft}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|12=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|12=draft}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|12=draft}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|12=draft}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|12=draft}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|12=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|12=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|12=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|12=draft}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line shrine|de|Shinto shrine (German)|ja|神社|zh|Shinto shrine (Chinese)|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|12=draft}}. == Enshrined Kami == * Main Enshrined Kami – {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto |lt_ja=建葉槌命 |lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German) |lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |fr|12=draft}} (たけはづちのみこと) * Local Tutelary – {{ill|Kagaseo (Mikaboshi)|de|Kagaseo (German)|ja|天津甕星|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi|2=zh|3=天津甕星|4=ja|5=天津甕星|6=ru|7=Амацумикабоси|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|zh|Kagaseo (Chinese) |lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=甕星香々背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|12=draft}} === About the Enshrined Kami === According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|de|Nihon Shoki (German)|ja|日本書紀|zh|Nihon Shoki (Chinese)|12=draft}} (Age of the Gods section), the two deities enshrined at {{ill|Katori Jingū|de|Katori Jingū (German)|ja|香取神宮|zh|Katori Jingū (Chinese)|12=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Shimousa Province|de|Shimousa Province (German)|ja|下総国|zh|Shimousa Province (Chinese)|12=draft}}) — {{ill|Futsunushi|de|Futsunushi (German)|ja|経津主神|zh|Futsunushi (Chinese)|12=draft}} — and at {{ill|Kashima Jingū|de|Kashima Jingū (German)|ja|鹿島神宮|zh|Kashima Jingū (Chinese)|12=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Hitachi Province|de|Hitachi Province (German)|ja|常陸国|zh|Hitachi Province (Chinese)|12=draft}}) — {{ill|Takemikazuchi|de|Takemikazuchi (German)|ja|タケミカヅチ|zh|Takemikazuchi (Chinese) |lt=Takemikazuchi |lt_ja=武甕槌神 |lt_de=Takemikazuchi (German) |lt_zh=Takemikazuchi (Chinese)|12=draft}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=香香背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|12=draft}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|12=draft}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|12=draft}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|12=draft}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|12=draft}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|12=draft}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|12=draft}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|12=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|12=draft}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|12=draft}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|12=draft}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|12=draft}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|12=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|12=draft}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|12=draft}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|12=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|12=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|12=draft}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|12=draft}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|12=draft}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|12=draft}} == Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === {{Infobox Shinto shrine |Name = '''Ōmika Jinja''' (大甕神社) |image = [[File:Ohmikahonden.JPG|280px]]
Honden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|36|30|49.8|N|140|36|45.6|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|12=draft}} 6-16-1 |Coordinates = {{coord|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=大甕神社}} |Main_deity = {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto |lt_ja=建葉槌命 |lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German) |lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|12=draft}} |Shintai = |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|de|Village shrine (German)|ja|村社|zh|Village shrine (Chinese)|12=draft}} |Established = (trad.) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|12=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|12=draft}}) |Honden = |Alternate_name = Ōmika Shizuri Jingū |Fudasho = |Annual_festival = {{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|12=draft}} |Rituals = Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki |label=Ōmika Jinja |caption= |coordinates= {{coord2|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E}} |position=left |width=220 |mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg |marksize=15 |float=center }} }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ōmika Jinja''' (大甕神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto shrine (German)|ja|神社|zh|Shinto shrine (Chinese)|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|12=draft}}. == Enshrined Kami == * Main Enshrined Kami – {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto |lt_ja=建葉槌命 |lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German) |lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |fr|12=draft}} (たけはづちのみこと) * Local Tutelary – {{ill|Kagaseo (Mikaboshi)|de|Kagaseo (German)|ja|天津甕星|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi|2=zh|3=天津甕星|4=ja|5=天津甕星|6=ru|7=Амацумикабоси|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|zh|Kagaseo (Chinese) |lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=甕星香々背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|12=draft}} === About the Enshrined Kami === According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|de|Nihon Shoki (German)|ja|日本書紀|zh|Nihon Shoki (Chinese)|12=draft}} (Age of the Gods section), the two deities enshrined at {{ill|Katori Jingū|de|Katori Jingū (German)|ja|香取神宮|zh|Katori Jingū (Chinese)|12=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Shimousa Province|de|Shimousa Province (German)|ja|下総国|zh|Shimousa Province (Chinese)|12=draft}}) — {{ill|Futsunushi|de|Futsunushi (German)|ja|経津主神|zh|Futsunushi (Chinese)|12=draft}} — and at {{ill|Kashima Jingū|de|Kashima Jingū (German)|ja|鹿島神宮|zh|Kashima Jingū (Chinese)|12=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Hitachi Province|de|Hitachi Province (German)|ja|常陸国|zh|Hitachi Province (Chinese)|12=draft}}) — {{ill|Takemikazuchi|de|Takemikazuchi (German)|ja|タケミカヅチ|zh|Takemikazuchi (Chinese) |lt=Takemikazuchi |lt_ja=武甕槌神 |lt_de=Takemikazuchi (German) |lt_zh=Takemikazuchi (Chinese)|12=draft}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=香香背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|12=draft}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|12=draft}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|12=draft}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|12=draft}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|12=draft}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|12=draft}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|12=draft}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|12=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|12=draft}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|12=draft}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|12=draft}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|12=draft}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|12=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|12=draft}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|12=draft}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|12=draft}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|12=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|12=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|12=draft}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|12=draft}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|12=draft}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|12=draft}} == Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Hitachi City History (Hitachi-shi){{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} == External Links == * [http://omikajinjya.sakura.ne.jp Ōmika Jinja] (Official Site) * {{Twitter|mikaboshi77|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Facebook|ohmikajinjya|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Instagram|omikajinja|Ōmika Jinja}} {{神道 横}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} (Chinese)|12=draft}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=香香背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|12=draft}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|12=draft}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|12=draft}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|12=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|12=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki {{coord2|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E}} |position=left |width=220 |mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg |marksize=15 |float=center }} }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ōmika Jinja''' (大甕神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto shrine (German)|ja|神社|zh|Shinto shrine (Chinese)|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|12=draft}} of {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|12=draft}}. == Enshrined Kami == * Main Enshrined Kami – {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto |lt_ja=建葉槌命 |lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German) |lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |fr|12=draft}} (たけはづちのみこと) * Local Tutelary – {{ill|Kagaseo (Mikaboshi)|de|Kagaseo (German)|ja|天津甕星|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi|2=zh|3=天津甕星|4=ja|5=天津甕星|6=ru|7=Амацумикабоси|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|zh|Kagaseo (Chinese) |lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=甕星香々背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|12=draft}} === About the Enshrined Kami === According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|de|Nihon Shoki (German)|ja|日本書紀|zh|Nihon Shoki (Chinese)|12=draft}} (Age of the Gods section), the two deities enshrined at {{ill|Katori Jingū|de|Katori Jingū (German)|ja|香取神宮|zh|Katori Jingū (Chinese)|12=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Shimousa Province|de|Shimousa Province (German)|ja|下総国|zh|Shimousa Province (Chinese)|12=draft}}) — {{ill|Futsunushi|de|Futsunushi (German)|ja|経津主神|zh|Futsunushi (Chinese)|12=draft}} — and at {{ill|Kashima Jingū|de|Kashima Jingū (German)|ja|鹿島神宮|zh|Kashima Jingū (Chinese)|12=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Hitachi Province|de|Hitachi Province (German)|ja|常陸国|zh|Hitachi Province (Chinese)|12=draft}}) — {{ill|Takemikazuchi|de|Takemikazuchi (German)|ja|タケミカヅチ|zh|Takemikazuchi (Chinese) |lt=Takemikazuchi |lt_ja=武甕槌神 |lt_de=Takemikazuchi (German) |lt_zh=Takemikazuchi (Chinese)|12=draft}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=香香背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|12=draft}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|12=draft}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|12=draft}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|12=draft}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|12=draft}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|12=draft}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|12=draft}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|12=draft}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|12=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|12=draft}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|12=draft}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|12=draft}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|12=draft}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|12=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|12=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|12=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|qq=draft}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|qq=draft}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|qq=draft}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|qq=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|qq=draft}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|qq=draft}} == Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Hitachi City History (Hitachi-shi){{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} == External Links == * [http://omikajinjya.sakura.ne.jp Ōmika Jinja] (Official Site) * {{Twitter|mikaboshi77|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Facebook|ohmikajinjya|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Instagram|omikajinja|Ōmika Jinja}} {{神道 横}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shizuri Faith}} [[category:Completion tier 1]] {{draft categories| [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmika Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmika Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Ibaraki Prefecture shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Hitachi City]] [[Category:History of Hitachi City]] [[Category:translated pages]] [[Category:Shizuri Faith]] [[Category:Shizuri Faith new pages]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmika Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōmika Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Ōmika Shrine]] [[ja:大甕神社]] [[de:Große Mikaschrein]]

revision.comment: 15 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:大甕神社 up to rev 102565296
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 大甕神社 |画像 = [[File:Ohmikahonden.JPG|280px]]
本殿 {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|36|30|49.8|N|140|36|45.6|E}}}} |所在地 = [[茨城県]][[日立市]][[大みか町]]6-16-1 |位置 = {{coord|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=大甕神社}} |祭神 = [[天羽槌雄神|建葉槌命]] |神体 = |社格 = 旧[[村社]] |創建 = （伝）[[皇紀]]元年（[[紀元前660年]]） |本殿 = |別名 = 大甕倭文(しず)神宮 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[5月5日]] |神事 = 式年大祭（御濱降神事） |地図={{Location map|Japan Ibaraki |label=大甕神社 |caption= |coordinates= {{coord2|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E|}} | position = left | width = 220 |mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg |marksize=15 | float = center }}}} {{座標一覧}} '''大甕神社'''（おおみかじんじゃ）は、[[茨城県]][[日立市]][[大みか町]]にある[[神社]]。 == 祭神 == * 主祭神 - [[天羽槌雄神|建葉槌命]]（たけはづちのみこと） * 地主神 - [[天津甕星|甕星香々背男]]（みかぼしかがせお） === 祭神について === 『[[日本書紀]]』神代に、[[下総国]]一宮である[[香取神宮]]の祭神[[経津主神]]と[[常陸国]]一宮である[[鹿島神宮]]の祭神[[タケミカヅチ|武甕槌神]]の二柱の神が邪神をことごとく平定したが、星の神の[[天津甕星|香香背男]]だけは従わなかった。そこで[[天羽槌雄神|倭文神建葉槌命]]が使わされ、これを服従させたと記されている{{Sfn|静神社（平凡社）|1982年}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}}。[[水戸藩]]神名録には当社は倭文神宮と記されている。伝説では、石名坂の峠の石が巨大化して天にまで届こうとしたのを、静の神が[[鉄]]の[[靴]]を履いて蹴ったところ石が砕け、かけらの一つが河原子(日立市)へ、もう一つが石神([[東海村]])に落ちたといわれている。柳田国男『日本の伝説』新潮文庫, ISBN 4-10-104702-2『日本の伝説』ではこの石を雷神石としているが、日立市史では雷神石と宿魂石は同じものとしている。かけらの飛んだ先は石塚（[[城里町]]）、石井([[笠間市]]）など諸説ある。 == 歴史 == 創建は社伝によれば[[神武天皇即位紀元|皇紀]]元年（[[紀元前660年]]）。最初は大甕山山上に祀られたが、[[1695年|元禄8年]]水戸藩主[[徳川光圀]]の命により甕星香々背男の磐座、宿魂石上の現在の地に遷座され、久慈、南高野、石名坂三村の鎮守とされた。この際に除地5石6斗8升6合の寄進を受けている。 == 境内 == 境内南東の[[鳥居]]をくぐると拝殿がある。拝殿の右奥にある岩山が、甕星香々背男の荒魂を鎮めたとされる宿魂石であり、その山上に建葉槌命を祀る本殿がある。宿魂石の北西側には甕星香々背男社がある。境内の北側、[[国道6号]]に面して大鳥居があるが、こちらは裏参道にあたる。境内社として稲荷神社、大杉神社、八坂神社、天満神社がある。 File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|拝殿 File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|宿根石上の本殿への参道　鎖場になっている File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|甕星香々背男社 == 祭事 == * 祈年祭（1月15日） * 節分祭（2月3日） * 例大祭（5月5日） * 甕星祭（7月7日） * 祖霊祭（8月12日） * おどう祭（秋季例祭）（11月2日-3日） * 新嘗祭（11月23日） * 式年大祭（御濱降神事）（[[申]]・[[寅]]の年）　令和４年より実行委員会主導で毎年斎行 === 御濱降神事 === 6年に1度、申・寅の年に行われる。[[神輿]]に御神体を移し氏子地域を巡行した後、久慈漁港で漁船に乗せて、海上を周回する。当神社の神輿は、[[天皇]]の[[高御座]]を模した八角形をしており、この大祭でのみ使われる。 令和４年より実行委員会主導で毎年斎行。 == 現地情報 == '''所在地''' * [[茨城県]][[日立市]][[大みか町]]6丁目16番1号 '''交通アクセス''' * 鉄道：[[東日本旅客鉄道]]（JR東日本）[[常磐線]] [[大甕駅]] （徒歩15分） * 車：[[常磐自動車道]] [[日立南太田インターチェンジ|日立南太田IC]]から4km == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * 日立市史（日立市）{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} == 外部リンク == * [http://omikajinjya.sakura.ne.jp 大甕神社]（公式サイト） * {{Twitter|mikaboshi77|大甕神社(大甕倭文神宮)}} * {{Facebook|ohmikajinjya|大甕神社（大甕倭文神宮）}} * {{Instagram|omikajinja|大甕神社}} {{神道 横}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおみかしんしや}} [[Category:茨城県の神社]] [[Category:日立市の建築物]] [[Category:日立市の歴史]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine |Name = '''Ōmika Jinja''' (大甕神社) |image = [[File:Ohmikahonden.JPG|280px]]
Honden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|36|30|49.8|N|140|36|45.6|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 6-16-1 |Coordinates = {{coord|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=大甕神社}} |Main_deity = {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto |lt_ja=建葉槌命 |lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German) |lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|qq=draft}} |Shintai = |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|de|Village shrine (German)|ja|村社|zh|Village shrine (Chinese)|qq=draft}} |Established = (trad.) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) |Honden = |Alternate_name = Ōmika Shizuri Jingū |Fudasho = |Annual_festival = {{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft}} |Rituals = Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki |label=Ōmika Jinja |caption= |coordinates= {{coord2|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E}} |position=left |width=220 |mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg |marksize=15 |float=center }} }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ōmika Jinja''' (大甕神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto shrine (German)|ja|神社|zh|Shinto shrine (Chinese)|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}}. == Enshrined Kami == * Main Enshrined Kami – {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto |lt_ja=建葉槌命 |lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German) |lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |fr|qq=draft}} (たけはづちのみこと) * Local Tutelary – {{ill|Kagaseo (Mikaboshi)|de|Kagaseo (German)|ja|天津甕星|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi|2=zh|3=天津甕星|4=ja|5=天津甕星|6=ru|7=Амацумикабоси|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|zh|Kagaseo (Chinese) |lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=甕星香々背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft}} === About the Enshrined Kami === According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|de|Nihon Shoki (German)|ja|日本書紀|zh|Nihon Shoki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (Age of the Gods section), the two deities enshrined at {{ill|Katori Jingū|de|Katori Jingū (German)|ja|香取神宮|zh|Katori Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Shimousa Province|de|Shimousa Province (German)|ja|下総国|zh|Shimousa Province (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) — {{ill|Futsunushi|de|Futsunushi (German)|ja|経津主神|zh|Futsunushi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} — and at {{ill|Kashima Jingū|de|Kashima Jingū (German)|ja|鹿島神宮|zh|Kashima Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Hitachi Province|de|Hitachi Province (German)|ja|常陸国|zh|Hitachi Province (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) — {{ill|Takemikazuchi|de|Takemikazuchi (German)|ja|タケミカヅチ|zh|Takemikazuchi (Chinese) |lt=Takemikazuchi |lt_ja=武甕槌神 |lt_de=Takemikazuchi (German) |lt_zh=Takemikazuchi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=香香背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|qq=draft}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|qq=draft}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|qq=draft}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|qq=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|qq=draft}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|qq=draft}} == Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === {{coord2|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E}} |position=left |width=220 |mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg |marksize=15 |float=center }} }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ōmika Jinja''' (大甕神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto shrine (German)|ja|神社|zh|Shinto shrine (Chinese)|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}}. == Enshrined Kami == * Main Enshrined Kami – {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto |lt_ja=建葉槌命 |lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German) |lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |fr|qq=draft}} (たけはづちのみこと) * Local Tutelary – {{ill|Kagaseo (Mikaboshi)|de|Kagaseo (German)|ja|天津甕星|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi|2=zh|3=天津甕星|4=ja|5=天津甕星|6=ru|7=Амацумикабоси|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|zh|Kagaseo (Chinese) |lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=甕星香々背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft}} === About the Enshrined Kami === According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|de|Nihon Shoki (German)|ja|日本書紀|zh|Nihon Shoki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (Age of the Gods section), the two deities enshrined at {{ill|Katori Jingū|de|Katori Jingū (German)|ja|香取神宮|zh|Katori Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Shimousa Province|de|Shimousa Province (German)|ja|下総国|zh|Shimousa Province (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) — {{ill|Futsunushi|de|Futsunushi (German)|ja|経津主神|zh|Futsunushi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} — and at {{ill|Kashima Jingū|de|Kashima Jingū (German)|ja|鹿島神宮|zh|Kashima Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Hitachi Province|de|Hitachi Province (German)|ja|常陸国|zh|Hitachi Province (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) — {{ill|Takemikazuchi|de|Takemikazuchi (German)|ja|タケミカヅチ|zh|Takemikazuchi (Chinese) |lt=Takemikazuchi |lt_ja=武甕槌神 |lt_de=Takemikazuchi (German) |lt_zh=Takemikazuchi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=香香背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|qq=draft}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|qq=draft}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|qq=draft}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|qq=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|qq=draft}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|qq=draft}} == Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === Site) * {{Twitter|mikaboshi77|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Facebook|ohmikajinjya|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Instagram|omikajinja|Ōmika Jinja}} {{神道 横}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|qq=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|qq=draft}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|qq=draft}} == Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Hitachi City History (Hitachi-shi){{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} == External Links == * [http://omikajinjya.sakura.ne.jp Ōmika Jinja] (Official
