{{Infobox Shinto shrine |Name = '''Ōmika Jinja''' (大甕神社) |image = [[File:Ohmikahonden.JPG|280px]]
Honden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|36|30|49.8|N|140|36|45.6|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 6-16-1 |Coordinates = {{coord|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=大甕神社}} |Main_deity = {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto |lt_ja=建葉槌命 |lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German) |lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|qq=draft}} |Shintai = |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|de|Village shrine (German)|ja|村社|zh|Village shrine (Chinese)|qq=draft}} |Established = (trad.) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) |Honden = |Alternate_name = Ōmika Shizuri Jingū |Fudasho = |Annual_festival = {{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft}} |Rituals = Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki |label=Ōmika Jinja |caption= |coordinates= {{coord2|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E}} |position=left |width=220 |mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg |marksize=15 |float=center }} }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ōmika Jinja''' (大甕神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto shrine (German)|ja|神社|zh|Shinto shrine (Chinese)|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}}. == Enshrined Kami == * Main Enshrined Kami – {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto |lt_ja=建葉槌命 |lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German) |lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |fr|qq=draft}} (たけはづちのみこと) * Local Tutelary – {{ill|Kagaseo (Mikaboshi)|de|Kagaseo (German)|ja|天津甕星|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi|2=zh|3=天津甕星|4=ja|5=天津甕星|6=ru|7=Амацумикабоси|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|zh|Kagaseo (Chinese) |lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=甕星香々背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft}} === About the Enshrined Kami === According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|de|Nihon Shoki (German)|ja|日本書紀|zh|Nihon Shoki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (Age of the Gods section), the two deities enshrined at {{ill|Katori Jingū|de|Katori Jingū (German)|ja|香取神宮|zh|Katori Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Shimousa Province|de|Shimousa Province (German)|ja|下総国|zh|Shimousa Province (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) — {{ill|Futsunushi|de|Futsunushi (German)|ja|経津主神|zh|Futsunushi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} — and at {{ill|Kashima Jingū|de|Kashima Jingū (German)|ja|鹿島神宮|zh|Kashima Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Hitachi Province|de|Hitachi Province (German)|ja|常陸国|zh|Hitachi Province (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) — {{ill|Takemikazuchi|de|Takemikazuchi (German)|ja|タケミカヅチ|zh|Takemikazuchi (Chinese) |lt=Takemikazuchi |lt_ja=武甕槌神 |lt_de=Takemikazuchi (German) |lt_zh=Takemikazuchi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=香香背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|qq=draft}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|qq=draft}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|qq=draft}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|qq=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|qq=draft}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|qq=draft}} == Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Hitachi City History (Hitachi-shi){{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} == External Links == * [http://omikajinjya.sakura.ne.jp Ōmika Jinja] (Official Site) * {{Twitter|mikaboshi77|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Facebook|ohmikajinjya|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Instagram|omikajinja|Ōmika Jinja}} {{神道 横}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} (Chinese)|qq=draft}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=香香背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|qq=draft}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|qq=draft}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|1=Shirosato, Ibaraki|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|qq=draft}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|qq=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|qq=draft}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|qq=draft}} == Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Hitachi City History (Hitachi-shi){{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} == External Links == * [http://omikajinjya.sakura.ne.jp Ōmika Jinja] (Official Site) * {{Twitter|mikaboshi77|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Facebook|ohmikajinjya|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Instagram|omikajinja|Ōmika Jinja}} {{神道 横}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} Mikaschrein]]

no Mikoto (Chinese)|qq=draft}} |Shintai = |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|de|Village shrine (German)|ja|村社|zh|Village shrine (Chinese)|qq=draft}} |Established = (trad.) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki {{coord2|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E}} |position=left |width=220 |mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg |marksize=15 |float=center }} }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ōmika Jinja''' (大甕神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto shrine (German)|ja|神社|zh|Shinto shrine (Chinese)|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}}. == Enshrined Kami == * Main Enshrined Kami – {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto |lt_ja=建葉槌命 |lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German) |lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |fr|qq=draft}} (たけはづちのみこと) * Local Tutelary – {{ill|Kagaseo (Mikaboshi)|de|Kagaseo (German)|ja|天津甕星|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi|2=zh|3=天津甕星|4=ja|5=天津甕星|6=ru|7=Амацумикабоси|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|zh|Kagaseo (Chinese) |lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=甕星香々背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft}} === About the Enshrined Kami === According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|de|Nihon Shoki (German)|ja|日本書紀|zh|Nihon Shoki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (Age of the Gods section), the two deities enshrined at {{ill|Katori Jingū|de|Katori Jingū (German)|ja|香取神宮|zh|Katori Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Shimousa Province|de|Shimousa Province (German)|ja|下総国|zh|Shimousa Province (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) — {{ill|Futsunushi|de|Futsunushi (German)|ja|経津主神|zh|Futsunushi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} — and at {{ill|Kashima Jingū|de|Kashima Jingū (German)|ja|鹿島神宮|zh|Kashima Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Hitachi Province|de|Hitachi Province (German)|ja|常陸国|zh|Hitachi Province (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) — {{ill|Takemikazuchi|de|Takemikazuchi (German)|ja|タケミカヅチ|zh|Takemikazuchi (Chinese) |lt=Takemikazuchi |lt_ja=武甕槌神 |lt_de=Takemikazuchi (German) |lt_zh=Takemikazuchi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=香香背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|qq=draft}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|qq=draft}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|1=Shirosato, Ibaraki|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|qq=draft}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|qq=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|qq=draft}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|qq=draft}} == Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === {{coord2|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E}} |position=left |width=220 |mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg |marksize=15 |float=center }} }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ōmika Jinja''' (大甕神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto shrine (German)|ja|神社|zh|Shinto shrine (Chinese)|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}}. == Enshrined Kami == * Main Enshrined Kami – {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto |lt_ja=建葉槌命 |lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German) |lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |fr|qq=draft}} (たけはづちのみこと) * Local Tutelary – {{ill|Kagaseo (Mikaboshi)|de|Kagaseo (German)|ja|天津甕星|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi|2=zh|3=天津甕星|4=ja|5=天津甕星|6=ru|7=Амацумикабоси|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|zh|Kagaseo (Chinese) |lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=甕星香々背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft}} === About the Enshrined Kami === According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|de|Nihon Shoki (German)|ja|日本書紀|zh|Nihon Shoki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (Age of the Gods section), the two deities enshrined at {{ill|Katori Jingū|de|Katori Jingū (German)|ja|香取神宮|zh|Katori Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Shimousa Province|de|Shimousa Province (German)|ja|下総国|zh|Shimousa Province (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) — {{ill|Futsunushi|de|Futsunushi (German)|ja|経津主神|zh|Futsunushi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} — and at {{ill|Kashima Jingū|de|Kashima Jingū (German)|ja|鹿島神宮|zh|Kashima Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Hitachi Province|de|Hitachi Province (German)|ja|常陸国|zh|Hitachi Province (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) — {{ill|Takemikazuchi|de|Takemikazuchi (German)|ja|タケミカヅチ|zh|Takemikazuchi (Chinese) |lt=Takemikazuchi |lt_ja=武甕槌神 |lt_de=Takemikazuchi (German) |lt_zh=Takemikazuchi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=香香背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|qq=draft}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|qq=draft}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|1=Shirosato, Ibaraki|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|qq=draft}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|qq=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|qq=draft}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|qq=draft}} == Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === {{Infobox Shinto shrine |Name = '''Ōmika Jinja''' (大甕神社) |image = [[File:Ohmikahonden.JPG|280px]]
Honden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|36|30|49.8|N|140|36|45.6|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 6-16-1 |Coordinates = {{coord|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=大甕神社}} |Main_deity = {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto |lt_ja=建葉槌命 |lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German) |lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|qq=draft}} |Shintai = |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|de|Village shrine (German)|ja|村社|zh|Village shrine (Chinese)|qq=draft}} |Established = (trad.) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) |Honden = |Alternate_name = Ōmika Shizuri Jingū |Fudasho = |Annual_festival = {{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft}} |Rituals = Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki |label=Ōmika Jinja |caption= |coordinates= {{coord2|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E}} |position=left |width=220 |mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg |marksize=15 |float=center }} }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ōmika Jinja''' (大甕神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto shrine (German)|ja|神社|zh|Shinto shrine (Chinese)|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}}. == Enshrined Kami == * Main Enshrined Kami – {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto |lt_ja=建葉槌命 |lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German) |lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese) |fr|qq=draft}} (たけはづちのみこと) * Local Tutelary – {{ill|Kagaseo (Mikaboshi)|de|Kagaseo (German)|ja|天津甕星|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi|2=zh|3=天津甕星|4=ja|5=天津甕星|6=ru|7=Амацумикабоси|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|zh|Kagaseo (Chinese) |lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=甕星香々背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft}} === About the Enshrined Kami === According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|de|Nihon Shoki (German)|ja|日本書紀|zh|Nihon Shoki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (Age of the Gods section), the two deities enshrined at {{ill|Katori Jingū|de|Katori Jingū (German)|ja|香取神宮|zh|Katori Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Shimousa Province|de|Shimousa Province (German)|ja|下総国|zh|Shimousa Province (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) — {{ill|Futsunushi|de|Futsunushi (German)|ja|経津主神|zh|Futsunushi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} — and at {{ill|Kashima Jingū|de|Kashima Jingū (German)|ja|鹿島神宮|zh|Kashima Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Hitachi Province|de|Hitachi Province (German)|ja|常陸国|zh|Hitachi Province (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) — {{ill|Takemikazuchi|de|Takemikazuchi (German)|ja|タケミカヅチ|zh|Takemikazuchi (Chinese) |lt=Takemikazuchi |lt_ja=武甕槌神 |lt_de=Takemikazuchi (German) |lt_zh=Takemikazuchi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo |lt_ja=香香背男 |lt_de=Kagaseo (German) |lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|qq=draft}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|qq=draft}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|1=Shirosato, Ibaraki|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|qq=draft}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|qq=draft}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|qq=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|qq=draft}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|qq=draft}} == Footnotes == === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Hitachi City History (Hitachi-shi){{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} == External Links == * [http://omikajinjya.sakura.ne.jp Ōmika Jinja] (Official Site) * {{Twitter|mikaboshi77|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Facebook|ohmikajinjya|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Instagram|omikajinja|Ōmika Jinja}} {{神道 横}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{Shinto-stub}} Shrine]][[Category:Shizuri Faith]] }} [[sn:Ōmika Shrine]] [[ja:大甕神社]] [[de:Große Mikaschrein]]

On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|qq=draft}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|qq=draft}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|qq=draft}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|qq=draft}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|1=Jōban Line|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|qq=draft}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|qq=draft}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|qq=draft}} == Footnotes == === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Hitachi City History (Hitachi-shi){{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} == External Links == * [http://omikajinjya.sakura.ne.jp Ōmika Jinja] (Official Site) * {{Twitter|mikaboshi77|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Facebook|ohmikajinjya|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Instagram|omikajinja|Ōmika Jinja}} {{神道 横}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{Shinto-stub}} Honden {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=280|coord={{coord|36|30|49.8|N|140|36|45.6|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibaraki Prefecture|qq=}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi City|qq=}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmika Town|qq=}} 6-16-1 |Coordinates = {{coord|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=大甕神社}} |Main_deity = {{ill|Takehazuchi no Mikoto|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|lt_ja=建葉槌命|lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi no Mikoto|qq=}} |Shintai = |Rank = Former {{ill|Village shrine|de|Village shrine (German)|ja|村社|zh|Village shrine (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village shrine|qq=}} |Established = (trad.) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōki|qq=}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/660 BC|qq=}}) |Honden = |Alternate_name = Ōmika Shizuri Jingū |Fudasho = |Annual_festival = {{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/May 5|qq=}} |Rituals = Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki |label=Ōmika Jinja |caption= |coordinates= {{coord2|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E}} |position=left |width=220 |mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg |marksize=15 |float=center }} }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ōmika Jinja''' (大甕神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto shrine (German)|ja|神社|zh|Shinto shrine (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi City|qq=}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmika Town|qq=}} of {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibaraki Prefecture|qq=}}. == Enshrined Kami == * Main Enshrined Kami – {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|lt_ja=建葉槌命|lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|qq=}} (たけはづちのみこと) * Local Tutelary – {{ill|Kagaseo (Mikaboshi)|de|Kagaseo (German)|ja|天津甕星|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi|2=zh|3=天津甕星|4=ja|5=天津甕星|6=ru|7=Амацумикабоси|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|zh|Kagaseo (Chinese)|lt=Kagaseo|lt_ja=甕星香々背男|lt_de=Kagaseo (German)|lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagaseo (Mikaboshi)|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu-Mikaboshi|qq=}} === About the Enshrined Kami === According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|de|Nihon Shoki (German)|ja|日本書紀|zh|Nihon Shoki (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} (Age of the Gods section), the two deities enshrined at {{ill|Katori Jingū|de|Katori Jingū (German)|ja|香取神宮|zh|Katori Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori Jingū|qq=}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Shimousa Province|de|Shimousa Province (German)|ja|下総国|zh|Shimousa Province (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimousa Province|qq=}}) — {{ill|Futsunushi|de|Futsunushi (German)|ja|経津主神|zh|Futsunushi (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} — and at {{ill|Kashima Jingū|de|Kashima Jingū (German)|ja|鹿島神宮|zh|Kashima Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Jingū|qq=}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Hitachi Province|de|Hitachi Province (German)|ja|常陸国|zh|Hitachi Province (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|qq=}}) — {{ill|Takemikazuchi|de|Takemikazuchi (German)|ja|タケミカヅチ|zh|Takemikazuchi (Chinese)|lt=Takemikazuchi|lt_ja=武甕槌神|lt_de=Takemikazuchi (German)|lt_zh=Takemikazuchi (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo|lt_ja=香香背男|lt_de=Kagaseo (German)|lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu-Mikaboshi|qq=}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|qq=}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mito Domain|qq=}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iron|qq=}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shoes|qq=}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Village|qq=}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|1=Shirosato, Ibaraki|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroi Town|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirosato, Ibaraki|qq=}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasama City|qq=}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōki|qq=}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/660 BC|qq=}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1695|qq=}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mito Domain|qq=}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Mitsukuni|qq=}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/torii|qq=}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 6 (Japan)|qq=}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 15|qq=}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 3|qq=}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/May 5|qq=}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 7|qq=}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 12|qq=}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November 2|qq=}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November 3|qq=}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November 23|qq=}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monkey (zodiac)|qq=}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tiger (zodiac)|qq=}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mikoshi|qq=}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|qq=}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor of Japan|qq=}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibaraki Prefecture|qq=}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi City|qq=}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmika Town|qq=}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmika Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|1=Jōban Line|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōban Line (JR East)|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōban Line|qq=}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/East Japan Railway Company|qq=}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōban Expressway|qq=}} == Footnotes == === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Hitachi City History (Hitachi-shi){{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} == External Links == * [http://omikajinjya.sakura.ne.jp Ōmika Jinja] (Official Site) * {{Twitter|mikaboshi77|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Facebook|ohmikajinjya|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Instagram|omikajinja|Ōmika Jinja}} {{神道 横}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shizuri Faith}} {{translated Jinja |caption= |coordinates= {{coord2|36|30|49.06|N|140|36|45.07|E}} |position=left |width=220 |mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg |marksize=15 |float=center }} }} {{座標一覧}} '''Ōmika Jinja''' (大甕神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|de|Shinto shrine (German)|ja|神社|zh|Shinto shrine (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi City|qq=}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmika Town|qq=}} of {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibaraki Prefecture|qq=}}. == Enshrined Kami == * Main Enshrined Kami – {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|lt_ja=建葉槌命|lt_de=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|lt_zh=Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|qq=}} (たけはづちのみこと) * Local Tutelary – {{ill|Kagaseo (Mikaboshi)|de|Kagaseo (German)|ja|天津甕星|1=Amatsu-Mikaboshi|2=zh|3=天津甕星|4=ja|5=天津甕星|6=ru|7=Амацумикабоси|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|zh|Kagaseo (Chinese)|lt=Kagaseo|lt_ja=甕星香々背男|lt_de=Kagaseo (German)|lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagaseo (Mikaboshi)|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu-Mikaboshi|qq=}} === About the Enshrined Kami === According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|de|Nihon Shoki (German)|ja|日本書紀|zh|Nihon Shoki (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} (Age of the Gods section), the two deities enshrined at {{ill|Katori Jingū|de|Katori Jingū (German)|ja|香取神宮|zh|Katori Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katori Jingū|qq=}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Shimousa Province|de|Shimousa Province (German)|ja|下総国|zh|Shimousa Province (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimousa Province|qq=}}) — {{ill|Futsunushi|de|Futsunushi (German)|ja|経津主神|zh|Futsunushi (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}} — and at {{ill|Kashima Jingū|de|Kashima Jingū (German)|ja|鹿島神宮|zh|Kashima Jingū (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Jingū|qq=}} (the chief shrine of {{ill|Hitachi Province|de|Hitachi Province (German)|ja|常陸国|zh|Hitachi Province (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi Province|qq=}}) — {{ill|Takemikazuchi|de|Takemikazuchi (German)|ja|タケミカヅチ|zh|Takemikazuchi (Chinese)|lt=Takemikazuchi|lt_ja=武甕槌神|lt_de=Takemikazuchi (German)|lt_zh=Takemikazuchi (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=}} — subdued all the evil gods but could not force the star deity {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|zh|天津甕星|ja|天津甕星|6=zh|7=Kagaseo (Chinese)|8=tr|9=Amatsu Mikaboşi|10=mni|11=ꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨ-ꯃꯤꯀꯕꯣꯁꯤ|12=th|13=อามัตสึมิกาโบชิ|15=draft|lt=Kagaseo|lt_ja=香香背男|lt_de=Kagaseo (German)|lt_zh=Kagaseo (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsu-Mikaboshi|qq=}} to submit. Therefore, {{ill|Takehazuchi|de|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (German)|ja|天羽槌雄神|zh|Takehazuchi no Mikoto (Chinese)|fr|lt=Takehazuchi no Mikoto|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|qq=}} (the weaving deity, also called 倭文神建葉槌命) was dispatched and made Kagaseo yield.{{Sfn|Shizumi Jinja (Heibonsha)|1982}}{{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} According to the shrine registries of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mito Domain|qq=}}, this shrine was known as the Shizuri Jingū. A legend states that a rock on the ridge of Ishinazaka grew so large that it reached the heavens, and that the Shizuri deity, wearing {{ill|iron|de|iron (German)|ja|鉄|zh|iron (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iron|qq=}} {{ill|shoes|de|shoes (German)|ja|靴|zh|shoes (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shoes|qq=}}, kicked it. The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Village|qq=}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|1=Shirosato, Ibaraki|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroi Town|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirosato, Ibaraki|qq=}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasama City|qq=}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōki|qq=}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/660 BC|qq=}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1695|qq=}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mito Domain|qq=}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Mitsukuni|qq=}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/torii|qq=}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 6 (Japan)|qq=}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 15|qq=}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 3|qq=}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/May 5|qq=}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 7|qq=}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 12|qq=}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November 2|qq=}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November 3|qq=}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November 23|qq=}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monkey (zodiac)|qq=}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|1=Tiger (Zodiac)|lt=Tiger (寅)|2=ja|3=寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tiger (zodiac)|qq=}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mikoshi|qq=}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|qq=}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor of Japan|qq=}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibaraki Prefecture|qq=}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City The rock shattered, and one fragment fell in Kawarago (present-day Hitachi City) while another fell in Ishigami ({{ill|Tōkai Village|de|Tōkai Village (German)|ja|東海村|zh|Tōkai Village (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Village|qq=}}).Kunio Yanagita, “Nihon no Densetsu” (Japanese Legends), Shincho Bunko, ISBN 4-10-104702-2In “Nihon no Densetsu,” this rock is called the “Raijin Stone,” but the Hitachi city history treats the Raijin Stone and the Shukon Stone as the same. Other theories say fragments fell in Ishizuka ({{ill|Shiroi Town|de|Shiroi Town (German)|ja|城里町|1=Shirosato, Ibaraki|zh|Shiroi Town (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiroi Town|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirosato, Ibaraki|qq=}}), Ishii ({{ill|Kasama City|de|Kasama City (German)|ja|笠間市|zh|Kasama City (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasama City|qq=}}), among others. == History == According to shrine tradition, it was founded in (legendary) {{ill|Kōki|de|Kōki (German)|ja|皇紀|zh|Kōki (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōki|qq=}} 1 ({{ill|660 BC|de|660 BC (German)|ja|紀元前660年|zh|660 BC (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/660 BC|qq=}}), the first year of the reign of Emperor Jimmu. Originally, it was enshrined on top of Mount Ōmika, but in {{ill|1695|de|1695 (German)|ja|1695年|zh|1695 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1695|qq=}} (Genroku 8), the lord of the {{ill|Mito Domain|de|Mito Domain (German)|ja|水戸藩|zh|Mito Domain (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mito Domain|qq=}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Mitsukuni|de|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (German)|ja|徳川光圀|zh|Tokugawa Mitsukuni (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Mitsukuni|qq=}}, ordered the shrine to be moved to its current location over the sacred rock (“Shukon Stone”), said to be the dwelling place of Kagaseo (甕星香々背男). It was designated the tutelary shrine of the three villages of Kuji, Minami-Kōya, and Ishinazaka. At that time, the domain granted a fief of 5 koku, 6 to, 8 sho, and 6 go. == Grounds == Passing through the southeastern {{ill|torii|de|torii (German)|ja|鳥居|zh|torii (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/torii|qq=}} leads to the haiden (worship hall). To the right rear of the haiden is a rocky hill called the Shukon Stone, where the fierce spirit of Kagaseo was pacified; the honden (main hall) of Takehazuchi no Mikoto stands on top of this crag. On the northwest side of the Shukon Stone is the Kagaseo Shrine. On the north side of the precincts, facing {{ill|National Route 6 (Japan)|de|National Route 6 (German)|ja|国道6号|zh|National Route 6 (China)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 6 (Japan)|qq=}} is a large torii gate, which serves as the rear approach. Within the shrine grounds, there are auxiliary shrines dedicated to Inari, Ōsugi, Yasaka, and Tenman. File:Ohmikahaiden.JPG|Haiden File:Ohmikashukkonseki.JPG|Climb to the main hall atop the Shukon Stone (a chain is used to ascend) File:Ohmikamikahoshi.JPG|Kagaseo (甕星香々背男) Shrine == Festivals == * Kinensai ({{ill|January 15|de|January 15 (German)|ja|1月15日|zh|January 15 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 15|qq=}}) * Setsubun-sai ({{ill|February 3|de|February 3 (German)|ja|2月3日|zh|February 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 3|qq=}}) * Annual Grand Festival ({{ill|May 5|de|May 5 (German)|ja|5月5日|zh|May 5 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/May 5|qq=}}) * Mikaboshi-sai ({{ill|July 7|de|July 7 (German)|ja|7月7日|zh|July 7 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/July 7|qq=}}) * Soreisai ({{ill|August 12|de|August 12 (German)|ja|8月12日|zh|August 12 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 12|qq=}}) * Odō-sai (Autumn Grand Festival) ({{ill|November 2|de|November 2 (German)|ja|11月2日|zh|November 2 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November 2|qq=}}–{{ill|November 3|de|November 3 (German)|ja|11月3日|zh|November 3 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November 3|qq=}}) * Niiname-sai ({{ill|November 23|de|November 23 (German)|ja|11月23日|zh|November 23 (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November 23|qq=}}) * Shikinen Taisai (Omihangō Shinji) (Years of the {{ill|Monkey (zodiac)|de|Monkey (German)|ja|申|1=Monkey (Zodiac)|lt=Monkey (申)|2=ja|3=申|zh|Monkey (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monkey (zodiac)|qq=}} and {{ill|Tiger (zodiac)|de|Tiger (German)|ja|寅|1=Tiger (Zodiac)|lt=Tiger (寅)|2=ja|3=寅|zh|Tiger (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tiger (zodiac)|qq=}}) – From Reiwa 4 (2022), held annually under the steering committee === Omihangō Shinji (御濱降神事) === Performed once every six years (in years of the Monkey or Tiger). The {{ill|mikoshi|de|mikoshi (German)|ja|神輿|zh|mikoshi (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mikoshi|qq=}} carrying the enshrined deity is paraded through the parish area and then taken aboard a fishing vessel at Kuji Fishing Port to circle the sea. The shrine’s mikoshi is octagonal, modeled after the {{ill|Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|de|Takamikura (German)|ja|高御座|zh|Takamikura (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamikura (Imperial Throne)|qq=}} of the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|de|Emperor of Japan (German)|ja|天皇|zh|Emperor of Japan (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor of Japan|qq=}}, and is used only for this grand festival. As of Reiwa 4 (2022), it is now carried out annually under the organizing committee. == Local Information == '''Address''' * {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|de|Ibaraki Prefecture (German)|ja|茨城県|zh|Ibaraki Prefecture (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibaraki Prefecture|qq=}} {{ill|Hitachi City|de|Hitachi City (German)|ja|日立市|zh|Hitachi City (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi City|qq=}} {{ill|Ōmika Town|de|Ōmika Town (German)|ja|大みか町|zh|Ōmika Town (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmika Town|qq=}} 6-16-1 '''Access''' * By train: About 15 minutes on foot from {{ill|Ōmika Station|de|Ōmika Station (German)|ja|大甕駅|zh|Ōmika Station (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmika Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Jōban Line (JR East)|de|Jōban Line (German)|ja|常磐線|1=Jōban Line|zh|Jōban Line (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōban Line (JR East)|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōban Line|qq=}} of {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|de|East Japan Railway Company (German)|ja|東日本旅客鉄道|zh|East Japan Railway Company (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/East Japan Railway Company|qq=}} * By car: Approximately 4 km from the {{ill|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|de|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (German)|ja|日立南太田インターチェンジ|zh|Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitachi-Minami-Ōta Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Jōban Expressway|de|Jōban Expressway (German)|ja|常磐自動車道|zh|Jōban Expressway (Chinese)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōban Expressway|qq=}} == Footnotes == === Explanatory Notes === {{Notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Hitachi City History (Hitachi-shi){{Full citation needed|date=2019年1月}} == External Links == * [http://omikajinjya.sakura.ne.jp Ōmika Jinja] (Official Site) * {{Twitter|mikaboshi77|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Facebook|ohmikajinjya|Ōmika Jinja (Ōmika Shizuri Jingū)}} * {{Instagram|omikajinja|Ōmika Jinja}} {{神道 横}} {{茨城県の神社一覧目次}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shizuri Faith}} {{translated page|ja|大甕神社|version=102565296|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[sn:Ōmika Shrine]] [[sn:Ōmika Shrine]] [[Category:Ibaraki Prefecture shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Hitachi City]] [[Category:History of Hitachi City]] [[Category:Shizuri Faith]] [[sn:Ōmika Shrine]] [[sn:Ōmika Shrine]] [[Category:Shizuri Faith]][[sn:Ōmika Shrine]][[Category:Shizuri Faith]] }} [[sn:Ōmika
